title: Structure of the atom
summary: This lesson introduces the nuclear model of the atom, stemming from the Rutherford-Geiger-Marsden experiment. It covers nuclear notation ($^A_Z X$), the concept of isotopes, and the fundamental forces at play within the nucleus, particularly the strong nuclear force.
learningObjectives: Describe the Rutherford-Geiger-Marsden experiment and its conclusions about atomic structure.
Use and interpret the standard nuclear notation $^A_Z X$ to describe nuclides.
Define isotopes and identify them based on their proton and neutron numbers.
Explain the role, range, and properties of the strong nuclear force in ensuring nuclear stability.

simpleExplanation.title: Unveiling the Atomic Core
simpleExplanation.summary: We'll explore how scientists discovered the tiny, dense nucleus inside atoms and learn how to describe different atomic versions. This involves understanding the powerful force holding it all together against immense repulsion.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're in a completely dark, massive warehouse and you suspect a tiny, solid steel ball is hidden somewhere inside. To find it, you start firing thousands of tiny pellets in all directions. Most pellets fly straight through and hit the far wall, telling you the warehouse is mostly empty. But every now and then, a pellet ricochets back at you with great force. This surprising rebound reveals the exact location and tiny size of the hidden steel ball. This is precisely how Rutherford's experiment revealed the atom's tiny, dense nucleus.
steps: Begin with the Rutherford scattering experiment, where alpha particles are fired at a thin gold foil.
Observe the results: most particles pass straight through, a few are deflected slightly, and a very small number (about 1 in 8000) are deflected by more than 90 degrees.
Draw conclusions about the atom's structure: it is mostly empty space, with a tiny, dense, and positively charged nucleus containing most of the mass.
Describe this nucleus using standard notation ($^A_Z X$) and understand the balance of forces (strong nuclear vs. electrostatic) that determines its stability.

flashcards.back: A particle found in the atomic nucleus. It can be either a proton or a neutron.
The number of protons in an atom's nucleus. It is also known as the atomic number and defines the element.
The total number of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus. It is also known as the mass number.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between A, Z, and the neutron number (N)?
flashcards.back: The neutron number is the difference between the nucleon number and the proton number: $N = A - Z$.
A specific type of atomic nucleus, characterized by its particular proton number (Z) and neutron number (N).
Nuclides that have the same number of protons (same Z) but different numbers of neutrons (different N). For example, Carbon-12 and Carbon-14.

flashcards.front: What was the key conclusion of the Rutherford-Geiger-Marsden experiment?
flashcards.back: The atom consists of a small, dense, positively charged nucleus surrounded by mostly empty space through which electrons orbit.
A fundamental force that acts over a very short range (approx. $10^{-15}$ m) to hold nucleons together in the nucleus. It is attractive and stronger than the electrostatic repulsion between protons.

Chemical properties are determined by electron configuration. Since isotopes have the same number of protons, a neutral atom will have the same number of electrons, leading to identical chemical behaviour.
A helium nucleus, consisting of 2 protons and 2 neutrons. It has a charge of +2e. It was used as a probe in the Rutherford scattering experiment.

flashcards.front: Common Trap: What is the difference between Nucleon Number (A) and Neutron Number (N)?
flashcards.back: The Nucleon Number (A) is the TOTAL count of protons AND neutrons. The Neutron Number (N) is ONLY the count of neutrons. Don't mix them up; remember $N = A - Z$.

flashcards.front: Compare the range of the strong nuclear force and the electrostatic force.
flashcards.back: The strong nuclear force is very short-ranged, acting only within the nucleus. The electrostatic force has an infinite range, although its strength decreases with the square of the distance.

faq.q: If the strong nuclear force is so powerful, why don't all atomic nuclei just clump together?
faq.a: The strong nuclear force has an extremely short range, only being effective over distances of about $10^{-15}$ metres. This is roughly the diameter of a nucleon. Outside of the nucleus, its strength drops to virtually zero, so it cannot pull nucleons from other, separate atoms.

faq.q: Why do neutrons help to hold the nucleus together?
faq.a: Neutrons play a crucial role in nuclear stability. They experience the attractive strong nuclear force with both protons and other neutrons, but they do not have any electric charge. Therefore, they add to the total attractive force holding the nucleus together without adding to the electrostatic repulsion that is trying to push it apart. This is why stable, larger nuclei always have more neutrons than protons.

An atomic mass unit (u) is a unit of mass used to express atomic and molecular weights. It is defined as one-twelfth of the mass of an unbound neutral atom of carbon-12 in its nuclear and electronic ground state. Approximately, $1 \text{ u} \approx 1.661 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$.

sections.content: Welcome to our exploration of the atom's core. For a long time, scientists pictured the atom as a 'plum pudding' – a diffuse cloud of positive charge with electrons embedded within it. However, a groundbreaking experiment in the early 20th century shattered this model and gave us the picture of the atom we use today: a tiny, dense nucleus orbited by electrons. In this lesson, we'll dissect that experiment and learn the language used to describe the rich variety of atomic nuclei.

In 1909, Hans Geiger and Ernest Marsden, under the direction of Ernest Rutherford, conducted an experiment that revolutionised our understanding of the atom. They fired a beam of alpha particles (which are helium nuclei, $^4_2He^{2+}$) at a very thin sheet of gold foil. A circular detector screen coated with zinc sulphide was placed around the foil, which would produce a small flash of light (a scintillation) whenever it was struck by an alpha particle.

Based on the prevailing 'plum pudding' model, they expected the alpha particles to pass straight through the foil with minimal deviation. While this was true for the vast majority of particles, they were astonished to observe that a small number were deflected at large angles, and a very tiny fraction (about 1 in 8000) even bounced back towards the source. Rutherford famously remarked, "It was almost as incredible as if you fired a 15-inch shell at a piece of tissue paper and it came back and hit you."

items: **Observation 1:** Most alpha particles passed straight through. **Conclusion:** The atom is mostly empty space.

**Observation 2:** A small fraction of alpha particles were deflected by large angles. **Conclusion:** There is a concentration of positive charge in the atom, as it repelled the positive alpha particles.
**Observation 3:** A very small number of alpha particles rebounded. **Conclusion:** The centre of positive charge (the nucleus) is very small, dense, and contains most of the atom's mass.

sections.content: The nucleus is composed of two types of particles, collectively known as nucleons: protons and neutrons. The number of protons defines the element, while the number of neutrons can vary. We use a standard notation to describe a specific nucleus, or nuclide.

Here, $X$ is the chemical symbol for the element. $Z$ is the **proton number** (or atomic number), representing the number of protons. $A$ is the **nucleon number** (or mass number), representing the total number of protons and neutrons. The number of neutrons, $N$, can be found easily by the relation $N = A - Z$.

sections.question: A nuclide of uranium is represented by $^{235}_{92}U$. Determine the number of (i) protons, (ii) neutrons, and (iii) nucleons in this nuclide.
sections.solution:
We use the notation $^A_Z X$.

(i) The proton number, $Z$, is the subscript. For $^{235}_{92}U$, the proton number is 92.
Number of protons = 92 ✓

(ii) The nucleon number, $A$, is the superscript, which is 235. The neutron number is $N = A - Z$.
$N = 235 - 92 = 143$
Number of neutrons = 143 ✓

(iii) The nucleon number, $A$, is the total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons).
Number of nucleons = 235 ✓

sections.content: Isotopes are nuclides that share the same proton number ($Z$) but have different nucleon numbers ($A$). This means they are atoms of the same element but with a different number of neutrons in their nucleus. For example, the element carbon ($Z=6$) exists in several isotopic forms, including Carbon-12 ($^{12}_6C$), Carbon-13 ($^{13}_6C$), and Carbon-14 ($^{14}_6C$). Because they have the same number of protons, and therefore the same number of electrons in their neutral state, isotopes have identical chemical properties. However, their different masses and neutron-to-proton ratios give them different physical properties, such as nuclear stability and density.

Consider the nucleus of a helium atom, which contains two positively charged protons packed into an incredibly small volume. According to Coulomb's Law, these protons should exert a massive electrostatic repulsive force on each other, causing the nucleus to fly apart. The fact that nuclei are stable implies the existence of another, even stronger, attractive force. This is the **strong nuclear force**.

items: **Strength:** It is the strongest of the four fundamental forces, but only over its specific range.
**Range:** It is extremely short-ranged, acting only over distances comparable to the size of a proton (around $10^{-15}$ m). Beyond this range, its effect is essentially zero.
**Action:** It acts between all nucleons: proton-proton, neutron-neutron, and proton-neutron.
**Charge Independence:** The force is independent of electric charge.

sections.content: In Paper 2, you may be asked to compare the properties of the strong nuclear force and the electrostatic force within a nucleus. A good answer will mention their relative strengths (strong force is stronger), their nature (strong force is attractive, electrostatic is repulsive between protons), and their range (strong force is short-range, electrostatic is long-range).

sections.question:
Two common isotopes of chlorine are Chlorine-35 ($^{35}_{17}Cl$) and Chlorine-37 ($^{37}_{17}Cl$).

(a) State the number of neutrons in a nucleus of each isotope.
(b) Compare the electrostatic force and the strong nuclear force acting between two protons within a Chlorine-35 nucleus.
sections.solution:
(a) For any nuclide $^A_Z X$, the neutron number is $N = A - Z$.

For $^{35}_{17}Cl$: 
$N = 35 - 17 = 18$ neutrons. ✓

For $^{37}_{17}Cl$: 
$N = 37 - 17 = 20$ neutrons. ✓

(b) Comparison of forces between two protons in the nucleus:

*   **Nature:** The electrostatic force is repulsive, while the strong nuclear force is attractive. ✓
*   **Strength:** At the typical separation distances within the nucleus, the strong nuclear force is significantly stronger than the electrostatic force. ✓
*   **Range:** The strong nuclear force is a short-range force, effective only within the nucleus. The electrostatic force is a long-range force (infinite range). ✓
*(Note: A good comparison requires identifying points of similarity or difference. Simply stating properties of each force separately may not gain full marks.)*
